coredata.dc:title: Structure–property relationships in fluorescence of carbon dots from premixed ethylene flames
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Combustion Institute. Published by Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Combustion Institute. Published by Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Shen, Chang
Zhou, Yuxin
Shao, Sen
You, Xiaoqing
coredata.dc:description: Structure–property relationships are essential for the efficient synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots by combustion–based methods. In this work, the structure–property relationships in fluorescence of carbon dots from premixed ethylene flames are investigated at different flame temperatures by applying both characterization methods and density functional theory (DFT) calculations. It is found that the fluorescence peak is blue–shifted and the quantum yield increases with higher flame temperature for carbon dots synthesized within the range of 1680–1770 K. Higher flame temperature can produce larger carbon dots with larger polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) and their derivatives inside. The –OH content and the C–O/C=O ratio are higher in the high temperature sample. As precursors of carbon dots, PAH derivatives with C–O have larger HOMO–LUMO gaps compared with PAH derivatives with C=O, leading to the blue shift of the 1π→π* transition in the UV–Vis spectra. It can be concluded that the conventional size effect is not the dominant factor controlling the blue–shifted fluorescence peak of carbon dots synthesized with higher flame temperature range of 1680–1770 K. More –OH in carbon dots from higher temperature flames can possibly lead to the higher quantum yield. The higher C–O/C=O ratio in carbon dots from higher flame temperature is likely to be the dominant factor governing the blue–shifted peaks in both UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra since it can enlarge the HOMO–LUMO gap and cause a blue shift of the 1π→π* transition.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Soot
Structure–property relationships
Oxygen–containing functional groups
HOMO-LUMO gap

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent properties of flame-formed carbon dots. 2(a)&(b) Photos of carbon dots prepared by different flame temperatures under day light and 365 nm UV light, (c) fluorescence spectra of carbon dots prepared by different flame temperatures (QY means quantum yield).
Morphology and carbon structures of flame-formed carbon dots. (a)&(b) HRTEM images of the Low Temp and High Temp samples, (c)&(d) Raman spectra of the Low Temp and High Temp samples.

Functional groups and electronic structures of flame-formed carbon dots. (a) FTIR analysis of the Low Temp and High Temp samples, (b)&(c) O 1 s of XPS from the Low Temp and High Temp samples, (d) HOMO–LUMO Gaps of coronene derivatives with different oxygen–containing functional groups, (e) computed UV–Vis of coronenyl–COOH and coronenyl–OH, (f) measured UV–Vis of the Low Temp and High Temp samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Simulated by USC Mech II at H p=2.0 cm.

head.ce:title: Structure–property relationships in fluorescence of carbon dots from premixed ethylene flames

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Combustion Energy, Tsinghua University, Beijing, 100084, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aCenter for Combustion Energy, Tsinghua University, Beijing, 100084, China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bKey Laboratory for Thermal Science and Power Engineering of Ministry of Education, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Structure–property relationships are essential for the efficient synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots by combustion–based methods. In this work, the structure–property relationships in fluorescence of carbon dots from premixed ethylene flames are investigated at different flame temperatures by applying both characterization methods and density functional theory (DFT) calculations. It is found that the fluorescence peak is blue–shifted and the quantum yield increases with higher flame temperature for carbon dots synthesized within the range of 1680–1770 K. Higher flame temperature can produce larger carbon dots with larger polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) and their derivatives inside. The –OH content and the C–O/C=O ratio are higher in the high temperature sample. As precursors of carbon dots, PAH derivatives with C–O have larger HOMO–LUMO gaps compared with PAH derivatives with C=O, leading to the blue shift of the 1π→π* transition in the UV–Vis spectra. It can be concluded that the conventional size effect is not the dominant factor controlling the blue–shifted fluorescence peak of carbon dots synthesized with higher flame temperature range of 1680–1770 K. More –OH in carbon dots from higher temperature flames can possibly lead to the higher quantum yield. The higher C–O/C=O ratio in carbon dots from higher flame temperature is likely to be the dominant factor governing the blue–shifted peaks in both UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra since it can enlarge the HOMO–LUMO gap and cause a blue shift of the 1π→π* transition.

ce:section.ce:para: Soot as combustion–formed carbon nanoparticles has been recognized as an atmospheric pollutant [1,2]. Numerous studies have been conducted from theoretical, experimental, and modeling perspectives to investigate the process of soot formation [3,4]. Particles below 10 nm are of particular importance in soot research [5], and have been studied using a variety of on-line diagnostic techniques, with a focus on the study of soot inception [6-12]. As functional nanomaterials, soot-derived nanomaterials below 10 nm have not been fully developed. The carbon black used as reinforcing filler in the tire industry is usually above 10 nm [13]. However, the emerging concept of carbon dots is driving research interest in this area.

Carbon dots are 2–10 nm carbon–based quantum dots that can emit fluorescence in various colors [14]. The initial discovery of the luminescence behavior of carbon dots came from Xu et al. in 2004 [15]. This class of materials was known as fluorescent carbon nanoparticles, graphene quantum dots, carbon quantum dots, carbon nanodots, carbonized polymer dot, etc. [16,17] before the concept of carbon dots was widely accepted [18]. Carbon dots are potential functional materials in lasers, solar cells, light–emitting devices, bioimaging, etc. [19-21]. Compared with traditional quantum dots (e.g. CdS [22] and CsPbX3 [23]), carbon dots are free from toxic Cd and Pb elements and have the potential to achieve low cost, high stability and environmental friendliness [24]. Liu et al. [25] utilized carbon dots to feed silkworms, directly demonstrating their low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility.

The traditional fabrication technique of carbon dots mainly lies in solvothermal method [26]. However, this wet chemistry method suffers from problems such as elevated pressure, massive quantities of organic waste liquid, noncontinuous and time consuming process, etc. [27] Combustion–based methods have the potential to overcome the aforementioned disadvantages [28]. It is not a new method since Liu et al. [29] investigated the fluorescent carbon nanoparticles derived from candle soot in 2007. After that, many researchers also utilized combustion–based methods to extract carbon dots from natural gas soot [30], pool fire soot [31,32], tire soot [33], cigarette smoke [34] and other solid waste carbon fumes [35]. However, the low quantum yield and unstable quality have been the major challenge. In 2019, Liu et al. [36] and Russo et al. [37] applied lab-scale flame reactors to investigate soot–derived carbon dots. This made it possible to control the combustion process. Russo et al. [38-41] pioneered the systematic research of fluorescent carbon dots from flames and analyzed the components of different molecular weights by size exclusion chromatography. Although we have known from solvothermal methods with polycondensation, crosslinking and carbonization processes [42,43] that the carbon core and surface functional groups [44,45] of carbon dots are crucial to the optical properties, the structure–property relationships in the fluorescence of flame–formed carbon dots have not been fully understood, hindering the development toward high quantum yields and tunable fluorescence.

In this work, fluorescent carbon dots are synthesized by premixed ethylene flames under different flame temperatures to probe the structure–property relationships of flame–formed carbon dots. The morphology and carbon structure of the carbon dots synthesized from ethylene flames will first be examined through systematic characterization methods, followed by an analysis of oxygen–containing functional groups using a combination of computational and experimental approaches. The study will elucidate the structure–property relationships for the synthesis of flame–formed carbon dots with high quantum yield and tunable fluorescence.

As shown in Fig. 1 , the carbon dots were prepared by a commercial McKenna burner consisted of a 60 mm diameter inner burner frit and a 5.5 mm width outer shroud ring frit, similar to our previous work [46,47]. The flow rates of C2H4, O2 and shroud N2 were controlled by mass flow controllers (MFCs). All MFCs were calibrated by the mini–BUCK calibrator with 0.5 % accuracy. A stagnation plate consisting of a water–cooled plate and a clean aluminum sheet beneath it was placed above the burner surface to capture carbon dots from the flames. The separation distance (H p) was set to 20 mm. The burner was cooled by water at 25±5 °C and the stagnation plate was kept at 400±40 K by cooling water at 85±5 °C to avoid H2O condensation and allow the thermophoretic collection of carbon dots from the flames. After collection, the black aluminium sheet with the carbon nanoparticles attached was scraped to obtain the soot powder containing carbon dots. 10 mg of powder was first extracted with 20 mL of analytical grade N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) and then filtered sequentially through a 220 nm PTFE membrane and a 20 nm Al2O3 membrane [37-41] to obtain ∼0.1 mg/mL fluorescent carbon dots dispersed in NMP.

The C2H4/O2 premixed flame conditions are shown in Table 1 . v 0 is the cold gas velocity indicating the flow rate. Φ is the equivalence ratio. T f, max is the maximum flame temperature, which is the characteristic parameter of standard McKenna flames and does not vary with the distance between the burner surface and the stagnation plate. The position of T f, max is 0.30±0.05 cm above the burner for all the flames we studied. Three sets of flames with incremental temperatures, denoted Low Temp, Mid Temp and High Temp, were designed to study the structure–property relationships in fluorescence of flame–formed carbon dots, considering that soot properties such as functional groups would be tuned by flame temperatures [48,49]. The uncertainty of T f, max is ±60 K [47,50], which is confirmed by the experimental data from Ciajolo et al. [51] and Apicella et al. [52].

The estimated yield of carbon dots on the consumed fuel is ∼1 %. The energy utilization efficiency is ∼100 times higher than traditional hydrothermal methods, which require ∼50 L of organic solvent for purification and ∼3 MPa high pressure conditions [27]. In contrast, the flame-based method requires ∼100 mL and operates at ∼0.1 MPa. This makes it an economical and sustainable process route for carbon dot production.

The fluorescence of the synthesized carbon dots was evaluated using a FluoroMax Plus spectrofluorometer by Horiba. Quantum yields were measured by the ratio of emitted photons to the absorbed photons using the integrating sphere. Elemental analysis was performed on Vario Micro by Elementar for all collected particles. High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was used for morphology analysis on Talos F200S by FEI. The filtered NMP dispersion of carbon dots was first treated by ultrasonic vibration for 6 h and the supernatant was dropped onto the carbon film as a sample ready for HRTEM analysis. Carbon structure analysis was carried out using Raman spectra from LabRAM HR Evolution by Horiba. Functional group analysis was conducted through Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy using Nicolet NEXUS 670 by Thermo Fisher. Quantitative measurement of oxygen chemical state on carbon dots was realized by X–ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) on Escalab 250xi by Thermo Fisher. Electronic structures of carbon dots from flames were analyzed by ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectroscopy on UV–3600 PLUS by Shimadazu.

In order to further analyze the electronic structures of carbon dots from flames on a molecular level, HOMO–LUMO gaps of coronene derivatives with different oxygen–containing functional groups were calculated at the B3LYP/Lanl2DZ level of theory [53], using Gaussian 16 [54]. Computed UV–Vis spectra were obtained via time–dependent density functional theory (TDDFT) at the same level of theory [55,56]. The B3LYP functional has been widely applied to calculate HOMO–LUMO gaps of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) and their derivatives [57,58]. The Lanl2DZ basis set was successfully applied by Veronesi et al. [53] to calculate PAHs and their derivatives from flames. The results were similar to those obtained with the 6–31G(d) basis set, with less than 1 % absolute deviation compared to the 6–311G++(d, p) basis set [59].
In this section, the fluorescent properties of the prepared carbon dots will first be analyzed qualitatively by human eyes and then quantitatively by a fluorometer, as shown in Fig. 2 .

It can be seen from Fig. 2(a)&(b) that the carbon dots dispersed in NMP appear transparent with brown color under day light and emit yellow–green fluorescence under 365 nm UV. As shown in Fig. 2(c), the fluorescence intensity gradually increases, accompanied by a blue shift in the fluorescence peak position (from ∼540 nm to ∼517 nm) with the increase of flame temperature. Moreover, the quantum yield also increases with the flame temperature, reaching 26 % for the High Temp sample. This value is competitive among carbon dots synthesized by other similar combustion–based methods [29-32,39]. Since the tendency of the temperature effect is consistent for all three samples, and also for the samples at Φ=2.7 and 3.0 in the range of ∼1650 K to ∼1750 K from our other results, the Low Temp and High Temp samples are selected for further characterization.

Table 2 lists the elemental composition of all collected particles in both samples. It is evident that they have similar composition with approximately 96 % for carbon, 1.5 % for hydrogen and 2.5 % for oxygen on a mass basis. As shown in Fig. 3 (a)&(b), the morphology of carbon dots from both High Temp and Low Temp samples exhibits particles with round shape under HRTEM. The particle diameter of the High Temp sample is larger at ∼3 nm compared to ∼2 nm for the Low Temp sample. Based on the Raman spectra presented in Fig. 3(c)&(d), it is observed that the intensity ratio I D1/I G of the High Temp sample is larger than that of the Low Temp sample, being 1.23 for the Low Temp sample and 1.32 for the High Temp sample. In regimes of high disorder or amorphousness, I D1/I G is positively related to the size of PAH islands inside one particle [60]. Thus, bigger PAH islands constitute bigger particles in the High Temp sample. It has been conventionally understood that both larger PAH islands [61] and larger particles [62,63] can cause a red shift in the fluorescence peak of carbon dots. However, from Fig. 2, the fluorescence peak undergoes a blue shift for the High Temp sample, which has larger PAH islands and particles. This indicates that the conventional size effect is not the dominant factor controlling the position of fluorescence peaks at different flame temperatures within 1680–1770 K.

In order to further explore the reasons for the blue–shifted fluorescence peak and elevated quantum yield of the High Temp sample, functional groups and electronic structure analysis are conducted in this section. Fig. 4 (a) shows the FTIR results of the High Temp and Low Temp samples [64]. The lower the transmittance peaks are, the richer the corresponding functional groups will be.

It can be qualitatively analyzed from the FTIR results that the primary differences between the two samples lie in –OH, C=O and aromatic C–H. Less aromatic C–H in the High Temp sample indicates its higher degree of maturity which is consistent with the results shown in Fig. 3(c)&(d). The increased content of –OH in the High Temp sample is likely to be the reason for its higher quantum yield, as –OH can act as an electron–donating fluorescent auxo chromophore that can enhance the fluorescence and increase the quantum yields of carbon dots [65]. A higher C=O content in the Low Temp sample indicates the presence of different kinds of oxygen–containing functional groups that may possibly explain the blue–shifted phenomenon of fluorescence shown in Fig. 2(c). To quantitatively analyze the content of oxygen–containing functional groups, Fig. 4(b)&(c) show the O 1 s spectra of XPS from both samples [66]. It can be seen that the C–O content is higher in the High Temp sample, increasing from 47 % to 65 %, and so is the C–O/C=O ratio, increasing from 0.89 to 1.86. To study the molecular structures via HOMO–LUMO gaps, coronene is selected as a representative PAH and its 8 derivatives with different C–O and C=O bonds are examined. The HOMO–LUMO gaps of these 8 species are compared in Fig. 4(d). It is evident that species with a C–O bond have a higher mean value of HOMO–LUMO gaps (3.59 eV, corresponding to the energy of 346 nm light) than those with a C=O bond (3.25 eV, corresponding to the energy of 382 nm light). This indicates that more C–O bonds in the carbon dots from higher flame temperatures could result in a blue shift of both the UV–Vis and fluorescence peaks. In order to further investigate the impact of C–O and C=O on UV–Vis of PAH derivatives, coronenyl–COOH and coronenyl–OH are chosen as representative ones for the calculations. As shown in Fig. 4(e), it is evident that C–O in PAH derivatives could cause a blue shift of the 1π→π* peak in UV–Vis, from ∼411 nm to ∼390 nm, as a result of greater HOMO–LUMO gaps. The 2π→π* peak remains the same at ∼323 nm for both coronenyl–COOH and coronenyl–OH. Fig. 4(f) depicts the measured UV–Vis spectra of the two carbon dot samples. The 1π→π* peak of the High Temp sample with more C–O is blue–shifted in comparison to the Low Temp sample, from ∼385 nm to ∼350 nm. The 2π→π* peak remains the same at ∼270 nm for both High Temp and Low Temp samples. These results are consistent with the general trend observed in the calculated UV–Vis spectra of the PAH derivatives as precursors for carbon dots. Thus, the C–O/C=O ratio can be the dominant factor controlling the blue–shifted phenomenon of both UV–Vis and fluorescence peaks for the High Temp sample since more C–O structures in High Temp sample can enlarge the HOMO–LUMO gap and cause a blue–shift of the 1π→π* transition.

In this work, the structure–property relationships in fluorescence of carbon dots from premixed ethylene flames are studied at different flame temperatures. It is found that the quantum yield increases and the fluorescence peak blue–shifts with higher flame temperature for carbon dots synthesized within 1680–1770 K. Higher flame temperature can produce larger PAH islands as building blocks to form larger carbon dots within 1680–1770 K. The content of –OH and the C–O/C=O ratio is higher in carbon dots from flames with higher temperatures. As precursors of carbon dots, PAH derivatives with edge H atoms of a PAH molecule replaced by C–O groups have larger HOMO–LUMO gaps compared to those by C=O groups, leading to the blue shift of the 1π→π* transition in UV–Vis spectra. The 1π→π* transition of carbon dots with more C–O from the High Temp sample is also blue–shifted in the UV–Vis spectra. It can be concluded that the convention size effect is not the dominant factor controlling the blue–shifted fluorescence peak of carbon dots synthesized by higher flame temperatures within 1680–1770 K. More –OH in carbon dots from flames with higher temperatures can possibly lead to the higher quantum yield. The higher C–O/C=O ratio in carbon dots from higher flame temperatures is likely to be the dominant factor governing the blue–shifted phenomenon observed in both UV–Vis and fluorescence peaks, since it can enlarge the HOMO–LUMO gap and cause a blue shift of the 1π→π* transition.

The novelty of this research is the successful synthesis of carbon dots with 26 % quantum yield and tunable fluorescence by a combustion–based method. The structure–property relationships of carbon dots from flames were systematically analyzed at different flame temperatures. It is significant because the quantum yield is competitive among other similar methods (typically below 15 %), and the structure–property relationships behind the tunable fluorescence peaks, which arise from the C–O/C=O content rather than the conventional size effect, were discussed by both characterization and calculation methods, which is novel and convincing.

Y. Z.: performed research, analyzed data
S. S.: performed research, analyzed data
X. Y.: designed research, analyzed data, wrote the paper

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China under grant number 2021YFA0716204 and the Space Application System of China Manned Space Program.
